1: tĕphillāh lĕmōsheh ʾîsh hāʾĕlōhîm — A Prayer of Moses, the man of God. The attribution to Moses makes Psalm 90 unique — the only Mosaic psalm in the Psalter. ʾÎsh hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God) is the title for prophets who speak God's word (Deuteronomy 33:1; 1 Kings 13:1; 2 Kings 4:7). ʾădōnāy māʿôn ʾattāh hāyîtā llānû bĕdōr wādōr — Lord, you have been our dwelling place in every generation. Māʿôn (home, habitation, dwelling place — the permanent residence of God as Israel's refuge across all generations; cf. Deuteronomy 33:27: mĕʿônāh ʾĕlōhê qedem, the eternal God is your dwelling). The psalm anchors the fleeting human life (vv3-10) against the eternal divine habitation.
2: bĕṭerem hārîm yullādû — before the mountains were born. Yālad (to bear, give birth) applied to the mountains — the creation of the mountains described as a birth event, personifying the earth's formation as parturition. wattĕḥôlēl ʾereṣ wĕtēbēl — or you had formed the earth and the world. Ḥûl (polel: to writhe in labor, bring forth in birth-pangs) — earth and inhabited world brought forth in a cosmic labor. ûmēʿôlām ʿad-ʿôlām ʾattāh ʾēl — from age to age you are God. The frame: creation events (mountains, earth) bracket the eternal divine being. God predates his own creation — the pre-creation God who will outlast it.
3: tāshēb ʾĕnôsh ʿad-dakkāʾ — you turn human beings back to dust. Dakkāʾ (a crushed thing, powder, dust — from dākāʾ: to crush) — humanity returned to the crushed, pulverized state of pre-formation. The reversal of the creation-act of Genesis 2:7 (wayyîṣer YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hāʾādām ʿāpār: from dust taken, to dust returned, Genesis 3:19). wattōʾmer shûbû bĕnê-ʾādām — and you say, 'Return, O children of Adam!' The divine word that reverses creation — shûbû (return) being the opposite of the forming act. Bĕnê-ʾādām (sons of Adam/humanity) — the collective solidarity of all human beings with the first human who was returned to dust.
4: kî ʾelep shānîm bĕʿênêkhā kĕyôm ʾetmôl kî yaʿăbōr — for a thousand years in your sight are like yesterday after it has passed. The divine perspective on time: a thousand years (ʾelep shānîm) as yesterday in retrospect (ʾetmôl kî yaʿăbōr: yesterday after it has gone by — i.e., past and therefore experienced as short). wĕʾashmûrāh balllāylāh — or like a single watch in the night. ʾAshm ûrāh (a watch — the ancient division of the night into three or four watches, each 3-4 hours). A thousand years equals the briefest human unit of experienced time — not that time is unreal but that the divine relationship to it is utterly other (cf. 2 Peter 3:8's use of this verse).
5: zĕramtām shēnāh yihyû — you sweep them away like a flood; they are like a dream. Zāram (to pour out in a torrent, sweep away — used of a rainstorm-flood; the sudden overwhelming inundation). Shēnāh (sleep) — human generations like a dream that dissolves on waking; no substance persisting after the divine gaze. baqqōqer kĕḥāṣîr yaḥălōph — in the morning they are like grass that sprouts. ḥāṣîr (grass, fresh vegetation — the type of rapid, ephemeral growth) that pushes up in the morning dew. The grass metaphor of Isaiah 40:6-8: all flesh is grass.
6: baqqōqer yāṣîṣ wĕḥālāph — in the morning it blossoms and shoots up. Yāṣîṣ (to blossom, flourish — the brief flowering of the grass) + ḥālap (to pass on, to shoot up quickly). lāʿereb yĕmôlēl wĕyābēsh — by evening it is mown down and withers. Mālal (polal: to be cut off, mowed down) + yābēsh (to dry up, wither) — the complete life-cycle of grass compressed into a single day: morning blooming to evening withering. The same imagery in Isaiah 40:7-8 applied to nations; in Ps 90 applied to human generations.
7: kî-khālînû bĕʾappekhā — for we are brought to an end by your anger. Kālāh (niphal: to be finished, brought to completion/end) — the divine anger as the agent that brings human existence to its terminus. ûbăḥămātĕkhā nibbhālnû — by your fury we are overwhelmed/terrified. Bāhal (niphal: to be dismayed, terrified, overwhelmed — the panic of sudden shock). The pairing of ʾap (anger, the flared nostril) and ḥēmāh (fury, hot wrath) — the two Hebrew terms for divine anger in increasing intensity.
8: shattāh ʿăwōnōtênû lĕnegdekhā — you have placed our sins before you. Shît lĕneged (to set before, display in plain sight) — the divine perspective sees what is hidden to others: the sins are placed lĕnegedekhā (before your face, in your presence). ʿălûmēnû lîʾôr pānêkhā — our hidden faults in the full light of your presence. ʿAlûm (hidden thing, secret fault — from ʿālam: to conceal) — what is concealed from human sight is exposed in the divine light (ʾôr pānêkhā: the light of your face). The divine omniscience as the context for human mortality.
9: kî khol-yāmênû pānû bĕʿebrātekhā — all our days slip away under your wrath. Pānāh (to turn away, to pass away — as a day turns and is gone). killînû shānênû kĕmô-hegeh — we bring our years to an end like a sigh. Hegeh (a sound, murmur, groan — from hāgāh: to murmur, meditate; the low sound of sighing, or possibly the growl of a lion, or the cooing of a dove — the onomatopoeic word for a brief, soft sound). Human years brought to an end with a murmur — not a shout but a barely audible exhalation. The restraint of the image makes mortality more, not less, poignant.
10: yĕmê-shĕnôtênû bāhem shĭbʿîm shānāh — the span of our years is seventy. The first statement of a human lifespan as a defined quantity — shĭbʿîm shānāh (70 years) + wĕʾim bigĕbûrōt shĕmônîm shānāh (and if strong, 80 years). These are not averages but ideals — the maximum ordinarily achievable without special divine extension. wĕrāhbām ʿāmāl wāʾāwen — but even then they contain only toil and grief. ʿĀmāl (toil, trouble, grievous labor) + ʾāwen (trouble, iniquity, emptiness) — the substance of the extended years. kî-gāz ḥîsh wannaʿûphāh — for they pass quickly and we fly away. Gāz (to cut off, pass quickly — the rapid passing); ʿûph (to fly away — as a bird takes sudden flight).
11: mî-yôdēaʿ ʿōz ʾappekhā — who truly grasps the force of your anger? The rhetorical question: who has really reckoned with the weight (ʿōz: strength, force) of the divine anger? ûkĕyirʾātekhā ʿebrātekhā — who fears your wrath as it deserves? Ûkĕyirʾātekhā (according to/equal to your being feared) — the fear the divine wrath is owed but not paid. The implication: no one fully grasps it, which makes the plea of v12 (wisdom to count our days) all the more necessary.
12: limnôt yāmênû kēn hôdaʿ — teach us to count our days aright. Limnôt (to count, reckon, number) — not mere arithmetic but moral reckoning: to assign each day its true weight knowing its finitude. Hôdaʿ (hiphil imperative of yādaʿ: teach, make known) — the wisdom-petition addressed to YHWH. wĕnābîʾ lĕbab ḥokhĕmāh — that we may acquire a wise heart. Lĕbab ḥokhĕmāh (a heart of wisdom) — wisdom located in the heart (the seat of understanding and decision), arrived at by the discipline of mortality-awareness. The psalm's intellectual core: the proper response to human finitude is not despair but wisdom.
13: shûbāh YHWH ʿad-māthāy — turn back, LORD! How long will this go on? The communal lament imperative — shûb (return, relent — the opposite of divine turning-away) + ʿad-māthāy (how long? — the standard lament formula). wĕhinnāḥēm ʿal-ʿăbādêkhā — have mercy on your servants! Nāḥam (niphal: to relent, be moved to compassion — the divine turning from judgment to mercy). The pivot from the meditation on mortality to petition: having stared into the reality of human finitude (vv3-11) and sought wisdom (v12), now the community cries out for divine relenting.
14: śabĕʿênû babbōqer ḥasdekā — fill us in the morning with your steadfast love. Śābaʿ (hiphil: to satisfy, fill — the same word used of Psalms 88:3 where the soul was filled with troubles; now the petition is to be filled instead with ḥesed). The morning as the time of grace: just as the grass springs up in the morning (v6), let the divine steadfast love spring up first. ûnĕrannĕnāh wĕnishmaḥāh bĕkhol-yāmênû — so that we may celebrate and rejoice through all our days. The goal: that the ḥesed filling the morning will sustain joy through the entire brief human lifespan.
15: śammĕḥênû kiymôt ʿinnîtānû — give us gladness to match the days you have made us suffer. The petition for proportional gladness — joy equal in measure to the suffering: kiymôt (like the days of) the affliction (ʿinnîtānû: you humbled/afflicted us). shĕnôt rāʾînû rāʿāh — joy equal to the years we have known trouble. The request is not for suffering to be denied but for it to be matched and ultimately overcome by gladness — a balance-of-years theology that assumes God can and will compensate.
16: yērāʾeh ʾel-ʿăbādêkhā phāʿŏlekā — let your work appear to your servants. Phōʿal (work, deed — the divine action in history) — the petition that the divine work become visible again to those who serve God. wahădarĕkhā ʿal-bĕnêhem — and your splendor shine on their children. Hādār (splendor, majesty, honor) — the divine glory that was seen in the Exodus and the wilderness now sought for the next generation (bĕnêhem: their children). The generational hope: the parents who suffered will not see the full manifestation, but their children will.
17: wîhî nōʿam ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhênû ʿālênû — may the favor of the LORD our God rest upon us. Nōʿam (pleasantness, grace, beauty, favor — from nāʿēm: to be pleasant, beautiful; cf. Naomi's name). The closing petition: the divine pleasantness resting on the community as its permanent atmosphere. ûmaʿăśēh yādênû kônĕnāh ʿālênû — establish the work of our hands. Kôn (hiphil: to establish firmly, make permanent) — the double petition with the doubly stated refrain: the human work (maʿăśēh yādênû) given permanence by divine action. The psalm closes where it opened: the permanent God asked to give permanence to what human hands create in their brief span.
